exercises.title: The 5 minutes before you code
exercises.instructions: What should you do in the first 5 minutes of a coding interview before writing any code?
exercises.expected_solution:
5 minutes before coding:
1. CLARIFY the problem (2 min):
   - Restate in your own words: 'So I need to...'
   - Ask about input constraints: size, range, null/empty?
   - Ask about edge cases: duplicates? negative numbers?
   - Ask about expected output format

2. EXAMPLES (1 min):
   - Walk through the given example
   - Create your own example (small, simple)
   - Think about edge cases

3. APPROACH (2 min):
   - State your approach BEFORE coding: 'I'm thinking of using...'
   - State time and space complexity
   - Get buy-in: 'Does this approach make sense?'
   - Consider alternatives if time allows

Why this matters: Interviewers evaluate your THINKING not just code.
Coding the wrong solution perfectly = worse than coding the right solution slowly.

test_cases.expected:

2. EXAMPLES (1 min):

   - Think about edge cases

3. APPROACH (2 min):
   - State your approach BEFORE coding: 'I'm thinking of using...'
   - State time and space complexity
   - Get buy-in: 'Does this approach make sense?'
   - Consider alternatives if time allows

Why this matters: Interviewers evaluate your THINKING not just code.
Coding the wrong solution perfectly = worse than coding the right solution slowly.

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Complexity communication script
exercises.instructions: Write a 30-second script you would say aloud to communicate the time/space complexity of your solution.
exercises.expected_solution:
Complexity communication script (30 seconds):

'Let me analyze the complexity of this solution.

Time complexity: This is O(n log n) because we sort the array once which takes O(n log n),
and then we do a single pass through the array which is O(n). The dominant term is n log n.

Space complexity: This is O(n) because we use a HashMap to store at most n key-value pairs
in the worst case where all elements are unique.

Could we do better? We could reduce time to O(n) using a counting sort since values are
bounded, but that would increase our space from O(n) to O(max_value). Given the constraints
mentioned, O(n log n) is acceptable here.'

Key pattern: [Complexity] because [reason]. Could we do better? [alternative + tradeoff]

test_cases.expected:
Complexity communication script (30 seconds):

'Let me analyze the complexity of this solution.

Time complexity: This is O(n log n) because we sort the array once which takes O(n log n),
and then we do a single pass through the array which is O(n). The dominant term is n log n.

Space complexity: This is O(n) because we use a HashMap to store at most n key-value pairs
in the worst case where all elements are unique.

Could we do better? We could reduce time to O(n) using a counting sort since values are
bounded, but that would increase our space from O(n) to O(max_value). Given the constraints
mentioned, O(n log n) is acceptable here.'

Key pattern: [Complexity] because [reason]. Could we do better? [alternative + tradeoff]

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: What to say when you're stuck
exercises.instructions: Script: You've been working on a problem for 8 minutes and are stuck. What do you say?
exercises.expected_solution:
When stuck: the 'stuck script'

Step 1: Acknowledge and verbalize (don't go silent):
'I want to think out loud for a moment. I'm exploring a brute force approach first...'

Step 2: Start with naive solution:
'The brute force here would be O(n²) — for each element, scan all others. Let me code this 
and then optimize.'

Step 3: Ask targeted questions (not 'give me a hint'):
'I'm thinking about using a HashMap for O(1) lookup — does that direction make sense?'
or 'I know this feels like a sliding window problem — am I on the right track?'

Step 4: Think aloud about patterns:
'This reminds me of [problem X]. The key insight there was [Y]. Let me see if that applies here.'

Step 5: Smaller example:
'Let me trace through a tiny example to build intuition: [1,2] with target 3...'

NEVER: Go silent for >90 seconds. Say 'I don't know' without elaborating.
ALWAYS: Keep talking, even if it's 'I'm stuck on X because of Y.'

test_cases.expected:

NEVER: Go silent for >90 seconds. Say 'I don't know' without elaborating.
ALWAYS: Keep talking, even if it's 'I'm stuck on X because of Y.'

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Naming and code style in interviews
exercises.instructions: What are the 5 most important code style rules for coding interviews?
exercises.expected_solution:
5 Code Style Rules for Interviews:

1. MEANINGFUL VARIABLE NAMES (never a, b, c except loop indices):
   Bad:  int x = ..., Map<Integer,Integer> m = ...
   Good: int complement = ..., Map<Integer,Integer> freqMap = ...

2. EXPLAIN EACH MAJOR STEP with a comment:
   // Step 1: Build frequency map
   // Step 2: Find first character with count == 1

3. HANDLE EDGE CASES FIRST (shows foresight):
   if (s == null || s.isEmpty()) return -1;
   if (k <= 0 || k > nums.length) return -1;

4. NO MAGIC NUMBERS — use named constants or explain inline:
   if (freq[c - 'a'] == 1)  // map char to 0-25 index

5. TEST YOUR CODE ALOUD before saying 'done':
   'Let me trace through with [1,2,3]: first iteration...'
   'Edge case: empty array — we return -1 due to the guard clause.'

Bonus: Write modular code — extract helper methods with clear names.

test_cases.expected:
5 Code Style Rules for Interviews:

1. MEANINGFUL VARIABLE NAMES (never a, b, c except loop indices):
   Bad:  int x = ..., Map<Integer,Integer> m = ...
   Good: int complement = ..., Map<Integer,Integer> freqMap = ...

2. EXPLAIN EACH MAJOR STEP with a comment:
   // Step 1: Build frequency map
   // Step 2: Find first character with count == 1

3. HANDLE EDGE CASES FIRST (shows foresight):
   if (s == null || s.isEmpty()) return -1;
   if (k <= 0 || k > nums.length) return -1;

4. NO MAGIC NUMBERS — use named constants or explain inline:
   if (freq[c - 'a'] == 1)  // map char to 0-25 index

5. TEST YOUR CODE ALOUD before saying 'done':
   'Let me trace through with [1,2,3]: first iteration...'
   'Edge case: empty array — we return -1 due to the guard clause.'

Bonus: Write modular code — extract helper methods with clear names.

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: The clarification question bank
exercises.instructions: List 10 clarification questions you should ask at the start of a problem. Categorize them.
exercises.expected_solution:
10 Clarification Questions by Category:

INPUT CONSTRAINTS:
1. 'What is the range/size of the input? 10^3? 10^5? 10^9?'
   → Determines if O(n²) is acceptable or need O(n log n)
2. 'Are inputs always valid, or should I handle invalid inputs?'
3. 'Can the array be empty or null?'

DATA RANGE:
4. 'Are values positive only, or can they be negative/zero?'
   → Critical for sliding window (negatives break it), two pointers
5. 'Can there be duplicate values?'
   → Affects set/map logic

OUTPUT FORMAT:
6. 'Should I return indices or values?'
7. 'If multiple answers exist, which one do I return?'
8. 'Is the output sorted?'

PROBLEM SPECIFICS:
9. 'Can I modify the input array in-place?'
   → O(1) space vs O(n) space solutions
10. 'Is this a streaming problem (one element at a time) or batch?'

Pro tip: You don't need to ask ALL 10. Pick the 2-3 most relevant to this problem.

test_cases.expected:

   → Determines if O(n²) is acceptable or need O(n log n)
2. 'Are inputs always valid, or should I handle invalid inputs?'
3. 'Can the array be empty or null?'

DATA RANGE:
4. 'Are values positive only, or can they be negative/zero?'
   → Critical for sliding window (negatives break it), two pointers
5. 'Can there be duplicate values?'

OUTPUT FORMAT:

PROBLEM SPECIFICS:

   → O(1) space vs O(n) space solutions
10. 'Is this a streaming problem (one element at a time) or batch?'

Pro tip: You don't need to ask ALL 10. Pick the 2-3 most relevant to this problem.

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Pattern recognition in 2 minutes
exercises.instructions: List the 8 most common interview problem patterns and their key signal words.
exercises.expected_solution:
8 Patterns and Their Signals:

1. TWO POINTERS:
   Signals: sorted array, palindrome, pair sum, remove duplicates
   → Start lo=0, hi=n-1 or both at 0 moving right

2. SLIDING WINDOW:
   Signals: subarray/substring, 'at most k distinct', 'longest without repeating'
   → Variable window: expand right, shrink left when invalid

3. FAST/SLOW POINTERS:
   Signals: linked list, cycle, middle, palindrome LL
   → slow=1 step, fast=2 steps

4. PREFIX SUM:
   Signals: subarray sum, range query, 'count subarrays with sum k'
   → prefix[i] = sum[0..i-1]; put 0→-1 in HashMap

5. MONOTONIC STACK:
   Signals: 'next greater element', histogram, daily temperatures
   → Maintain increasing or decreasing stack

6. BFS/DFS:
   Signals: tree traversal, shortest path, connected components, grid
   → BFS=queue+level, DFS=stack/recursion

7. DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING:
   Signals: 'minimum cost', 'maximum profit', 'how many ways', 'can you reach'
   → Optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems

8. BINARY SEARCH:
   Signals: sorted array, 'find minimum such that', rotated array
   → Binary search on answer space if feasibility is monotonic

test_cases.expected:
8 Patterns and Their Signals:

1. TWO POINTERS:
   Signals: sorted array, palindrome, pair sum, remove duplicates
   → Start lo=0, hi=n-1 or both at 0 moving right

2. SLIDING WINDOW:
   Signals: subarray/substring, 'at most k distinct', 'longest without repeating'
   → Variable window: expand right, shrink left when invalid

3. FAST/SLOW POINTERS:
   Signals: linked list, cycle, middle, palindrome LL
   → slow=1 step, fast=2 steps

4. PREFIX SUM:
   Signals: subarray sum, range query, 'count subarrays with sum k'
   → prefix[i] = sum[0..i-1]; put 0→-1 in HashMap

5. MONOTONIC STACK:
   Signals: 'next greater element', histogram, daily temperatures
   → Maintain increasing or decreasing stack

6. BFS/DFS:
   Signals: tree traversal, shortest path, connected components, grid
   → BFS=queue+level, DFS=stack/recursion

7. DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING:
   Signals: 'minimum cost', 'maximum profit', 'how many ways', 'can you reach'
   → Optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems

8. BINARY SEARCH:
   Signals: sorted array, 'find minimum such that', rotated array
   → Binary search on answer space if feasibility is monotonic

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Time management in a 45-minute interview
exercises.instructions: Create a time budget for a 45-minute coding interview with one medium problem.
exercises.expected_solution:
45-Minute Interview Time Budget:

0:00–2:00 (2 min) — CLARIFY
- Restate problem, ask 2-3 targeted clarifying questions
- Confirm input/output format and constraints

2:00–5:00 (3 min) — PLAN
- State your approach: 'I'll use X because Y'
- State complexity: 'This should be O(n log n) time, O(n) space'
- Get interviewer buy-in before coding

5:00–25:00 (20 min) — CODE
- Write clean, commented code
- Narrate what you're doing: 'Now I'm building the frequency map...'
- At 15-min mark: if not done, ask 'Am I on track for time?'

25:00–30:00 (5 min) — TEST
- Trace through your example manually
- Check edge cases: empty input, single element, all same
- Fix bugs you find

30:00–40:00 (10 min) — OPTIMIZE + DISCUSS
- Discuss time/space complexity
- Propose optimizations if time allows
- Discuss trade-offs: 'We could use X to get O(n) but it uses more memory'

40:00–45:00 (5 min) — QUESTIONS FOR INTERVIEWER
- Prepare 2-3 thoughtful questions about the team/product

30:00–40:00 (10 min) — OPTIMIZE + DISCUSS
- Discuss time/space complexity
- Propose optimizations if time allows
- Discuss trade-offs: 'We could use X to get O(n) but it uses more memory'

40:00–45:00 (5 min) — QUESTIONS FOR INTERVIEWER
- Prepare 2-3 thoughtful questions about the team/product

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Mock problem: Two Sum under timed conditions
exercises.instructions: Solve Two Sum as if in an interview. Write everything you would say aloud AND the code.
hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Handling follow-up questions
exercises.instructions: The interviewer asks 'Can you do this in O(1) space?' — how do you respond?
exercises.expected_solution:
Responding to 'Can you do this in O(1) space?':

Step 1: THINK ALOUD (don't panic):
'Good question. Let me think about whether O(1) space is possible...'

Step 2: ANALYZE CONSTRAINTS:
'Currently I use a HashMap for O(n) space. To get O(1) space, I'd need to either:
(a) Modify the input array in-place (is that allowed?)
(b) Use a mathematical property instead of storing values
(c) Use the array itself as a hash map (negative marking, etc.)'

Step 3: EXPLORE AND BE HONEST:
'For Two Sum with arbitrary values, I don't think O(1) space is possible in general
because we need to remember which values we've seen. If the array were sorted, 
we could use two pointers: O(1) space, O(n) time — but we'd need to track original indices.'

Step 4: OFFER ALTERNATIVES:
'We could sort first + two pointers: O(n log n) time O(1) space (or O(n) for sorted copy).
The trade-off is we lose the O(n) time guarantee.'

Step 5: ASK CLARIFYING QUESTION:
'Is modifying the input array acceptable? That opens up some in-place approaches.'

Key: Interviewers often ask impossible follow-ups to see HOW you think under pressure.

Step 3: EXPLORE AND BE HONEST:

because we need to remember which values we've seen. If the array were sorted, 
we could use two pointers: O(1) space, O(n) time — but we'd need to track original indices.'

Step 4: OFFER ALTERNATIVES:
'We could sort first + two pointers: O(n log n) time O(1) space (or O(n) for sorted copy).
The trade-off is we lose the O(n) time guarantee.'

Step 5: ASK CLARIFYING QUESTION:
'Is modifying the input array acceptable? That opens up some in-place approaches.'

Key: Interviewers often ask impossible follow-ups to see HOW you think under pressure.

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Debugging live: wrong answer
exercises.instructions: Your code produces wrong output for the test case [3,3] target=6. Walk through debugging.
exercises.expected_solution:
Debugging Live — Wrong Answer:

[STEP 1: Don't panic, narrate]
'Interesting — let me trace through [3,3] with target=6.'

[STEP 2: Trace manually]
'i=0: complement = 6-3 = 3. Is 3 in seen? No (map is empty). Add 3→0 to map.
i=1: complement = 6-3 = 3. Is 3 in seen? Yes, at index 0. Return [0,1].'

[STEP 3: Compare with actual output]
'Wait, my code returns... let me check. Oh! I'm using seen.containsKey(nums[i])
instead of seen.containsKey(complement). That's the bug — I was looking up the
wrong value.'

[STEP 4: Fix and re-verify]
'Fix: change seen.containsKey(nums[i]) to seen.containsKey(complement).
Now trace again:
i=0: complement=3. seen.containsKey(3)? No. Put nums[0]=3→0.
i=1: complement=3. seen.containsKey(3)? Yes! Return [0,1]. Correct!'

[STEP 5: Check original test cases still pass]
'Let me verify [2,7,11,15] target=9 still works:
i=0: complement=7, not in map. Add 2→0.
i=1: complement=2, in map at 0. Return [0,1]. Correct!'

Lesson: When debugging, trace the exact code line-by-line, not what you THINK it does.

test_cases.expected:
Debugging Live — Wrong Answer:

[STEP 1: Don't panic, narrate]

[STEP 2: Trace manually]

i=1: complement = 6-3 = 3. Is 3 in seen? Yes, at index 0. Return [0,1].'

[STEP 3: Compare with actual output]
'Wait, my code returns... let me check. Oh! I'm using seen.containsKey(nums[i])
instead of seen.containsKey(complement). That's the bug — I was looking up the
wrong value.'

[STEP 4: Fix and re-verify]
'Fix: change seen.containsKey(nums[i]) to seen.containsKey(complement).
Now trace again:
i=0: complement=3. seen.containsKey(3)? No. Put nums[0]=3→0.
i=1: complement=3. seen.containsKey(3)? Yes! Return [0,1]. Correct!'

[STEP 5: Check original test cases still pass]

i=0: complement=7, not in map. Add 2→0.
i=1: complement=2, in map at 0. Return [0,1]. Correct!'

Lesson: When debugging, trace the exact code line-by-line, not what you THINK it does.

hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: Optimize from O(n²) to O(n): live narration
exercises.instructions: Starting from an O(n²) brute force for 'find all pairs with sum k', optimize to O(n) while narrating.
hints: Use the STAR method where applicable

exercises.title: The 'I don't know the optimal solution' script
exercises.instructions: How do you handle it when you genuinely don't know the optimal solution to a problem?
exercises.expected_solution:
'I Don't Know the Optimal' Script:

[STEP 1: Show what you DO know]
'I can see a brute force solution that's O(n²). Let me code that first to show I 
understand the problem correctly, then we can optimize.'

[STEP 2: Think aloud about bottlenecks]
'The bottleneck is [X]. To optimize, I'd want to eliminate [X] by... 
Hmm, could I precompute [Y]? Or use a different data structure for [Z]?'

[STEP 3: Pattern-match honestly]
'This feels similar to a sliding window problem, but I'm not sure how to apply it here 
because of the [complication]. Could you confirm if I'm in the right direction?'

[STEP 4: Implement partial solution]
'I know how to handle the case where [simpler version]. Let me implement that,
and then we can extend it.'

[STEP 5: Ask for a nudge (gracefully)]
'I have a feeling there's an O(n) approach using [HashMap/Stack/DP], but I'm not 
seeing the connection. Is there a key insight I'm missing?'

What NOT to do:
- Go silent for more than 90 seconds
- Say 'I give up'
- Google on your phone
- Pretend you know when you don't

Interviewers respect intellectual honesty over false confidence.

Interviewers respect intellectual honesty over false confidence.

exercises.title: Final coding interview checklist
exercises.instructions: Create a 20-point pre-interview checklist for a Java coding interview at Zerodha/Flipkart.
exercises.expected_solution:
20-Point Pre-Interview Checklist:

KNOWLEDGE CHECK (verify you can code in 5 min):
✅ 1. Two Sum (HashMap approach)
✅ 2. Reverse linked list (3-pointer)
✅ 3. Valid parentheses (stack)
✅ 4. Level-order BFS (queue + size snapshot)
✅ 5. Binary search (lo+(hi-lo)/2 template)
✅ 6. Merge sort / quicksort
✅ 7. Dynamic programming (coin change, LIS)
✅ 8. DFS/BFS on grid (islands)

PATTERN READINESS:
✅ 9. Two pointers, sliding window, prefix sum
✅ 10. Fast/slow pointers, monotonic stack
✅ 11. Union-Find with path compression
✅ 12. Dijkstra with min-heap
✅ 13. Backtracking template (choose→recurse→undo)

COMMUNICATION SKILLS:
✅ 14. Clarification questions rehearsed (5 good ones)
✅ 15. Complexity communication script ready
✅ 16. 'Stuck script' ready
✅ 17. 3 behavioral stories prepared (STAR format)

LOGISTICS:
✅ 18. IDE ready (IntelliJ) OR LeetCode playground tested
✅ 19. Distraction-free environment confirmed
✅ 20. Pen + paper ready for whiteboarding if needed

DAY OF:
- Sleep 7+ hours
- Eat before interview
- Join 5 min early
- Take a breath before starting
